figures.caption: Fig. 5: Simulation benchmarks setups. We do simulation evaluation spanning generalist and specialist policies, with diverse embodiment, action space, and task.
TABLE VII: Sequence and execution parameters across environments.
TABLE VIII: Scoring rubric for real-world WidowX evaluation tasks. Scores represent cumulative progress

Fig. 6: BridgeV2 pretraining to real-world WidowX Zero-Shot Performance Comparison. As a property of VLA models, the released weights work out-of-the-box without an accurate requirement for the camera pose. We set the camera pose so that all methods reach a 100% success rate on an easy in-distribution task, then test them zero-shot on challenging tasks. Details of experiment protocol in Appendix.
TABLE VI: Comparative training configurations for generalist (Phases 1 & 2) and specialist policies.
TABLE V: Architecture specifications for Generalist (AR-VLA) and Specialist (AR-Actor) models. The specialist
Fig. 15: AR-VLA Zero-shot task execution in real world.
Fig. 14: AR-VLA Zero-shot task execution in SIMPLER simulator.
Fig. 9: Three different Action Experts sharing the same architecture and V-L backbone. The same networks are trained and used differently.
Fig. 10: AR Actor that share the exact same size and architecture of Action Chunking Transformer, the same decoders are trained and used differently.

Fig. 8: History-Awareness Evaluation. PushT2 requires visiting both goals, but which goal has been visited is unobservable midway. Stack3 requires stacking cups over a battery that becomes occluded. Both task require memory of unobservable past states. H donates the context window length of AR-VLA. Details about task defination, data collection, training and execution in Appendix.

TABLE IV: Ablation Study of AR-VLA Design Choice. Validate Error calculate from the model output and ground truth when taking dataset trajectory as inout. Success rates are percentages in total 96 SIMPLER trials after training. Bold indicates the full AR-VLA configuration.
Fig. 12: Typical cases during PushT2 task execution.
Fig. 13: Typical cases during Stack3 task execution.

Fig. 11: Demonstration collection for history-aware tasks. (a) The PushT2 task can be complete in two different orders, we don’t constrain human demonstrators on their solution. (b) We collect demonstrations for Stack3 under different initial object layouts. On stage 2, the visual snapshot is identical (the battery in dark is covered, and the camera can only see two cups) but the next object to pick is different.
